flashcards.back: An encoder is a combinational logic circuit that converts multiple input signals into a coded binary output, typically representing which input line is active.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a simple encoder and a priority encoder?
flashcards.back: A simple encoder assumes only one input is active at a time, while a priority encoder can handle multiple active inputs by assigning priority to the highest-order input.
A 4-to-2 encoder has 4 input lines and 2 output lines. When one input is active, the output provides a 2-bit binary code (00, 01, 10, 11) representing which input is active.

flashcards.front: What are common applications of encoders?
flashcards.back: Encoders are used in computer keyboards, interrupt handling systems, analog-to-digital converters, and any application where multiple inputs need to be converted to binary code.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between encoders and decoders?
flashcards.back: Encoders and decoders are complementary circuits. Encoders convert multiple inputs to coded binary output, while decoders convert coded binary input to multiple outputs.
